entry

entry

Declare a ProcScript entry, a script module that can be invoked from within the same component.

entry EntryName
{returns DataType}
{params
...
endparams}
{variables
...
endvariables}

... Proc statements and precompiler directives

{return (Expression) }
end
        

Parameters

EntryName—literal name of the entry; can have a maximum length of 32 bytes; these characters can be letters (A-Z), digits (0-9), or underscores (_).

Return Values

The entry statement does not affect the value of $status.

The value passed with the return statement is returned inline in case the function or entry is invoked inline and in $status when invoked using the call statement. When invoked using the call statement, the value is limited to integers only and any returns declaration is ignored.

Use

Allowed in all component types, as well as application shells and as global ProcScripts.

Description

The entry statement declares the name and the starting point of an entry with a visibility limited to the component it is declared in. It also declares the end of the previous ProcScript module, if its end statement was omitted). An entry must be declared in the same component as the code that calls it.

An entry must be declared in the same component as it is called.

Entries in Components

All entries in a component are treated as if they are defined in the component object, even if they have been defined at the entity or field level. This means, for example, that an entry with name MYENTRY defined at field level overwrites an entry with the same name defined in the same component at component level.

When an entry that is defined in a component has the same name as one inherited from the model, the one in the component takes precedence. If an entry is defined more than once in a component, the last one encountered is the one that is compiled into the component.

This can lead to unexpected behavior if you are not aware that there are multiple entries defined, or do not know where they are defined. You can use the Compiled Modules Inspector in the Uniface IDE to get insight into such a situation.
Note: We recommend that you use functions instead of entries, because functions do not have this behavior. See Functions.

Entries in Global ProcScript

Entries that are declared in Global ProcScripts have global scope and can be called from any application component.

Global ProcScripts are implicit entries, but they can also contain explicitly-defined entries. These must be placed at the end of all other code in the global ProcScript. Otherwise, the instructions making up the main global ProcScript code are ignored.

Calling Entries

Entries can be explicitly called using the call ProcScript statement, in which case any return value is returned in $status.

However, entries are like private functions that can be called from within the component, or in the case of Global ProcScript, from anywhere within the application. If they are called as a function, the return value can be assigned to a variable or used as an argument or parameter. This means that they can be used directly in the code.

It also means that the return value can be of a different data type than numeric (which is what $status is).

For example:

entry doSomething
 returns string ; set the data type of the return value 
 return("I did something") ; set the return value
end

This can be called:

  • As a function: 
    vResult = doSomething()
; vResult = "I did something"
; $status = 0
  • Using call: 
    call doSomething
; $status = 0

    In this case, the return value is not captured inline.

Example: Using Entries

In the following example, the store trigger contains a call to an entry in the component.

trigger store
call LSTORE
end; store

; Component-level Script container
entry LSTORE
 store
 if ($status < 0)
 message "Store error!"
 rollback
 else
 message "Store done."
 commit
 endif
end

Example: Using Entries as Functions

The following code calls entry multiply and assigns its value to TOTAL:

TOTAL = multiply(FLD1, FLD2)

; Component-level Script container
entry multiply
returns numeric
params
 numeric parm1 : IN
 numeric parm2 : IN
endparams
variables
 numeric multiplyResult
endvariables
multiplyResult = parm1 * parm2
return multiplyResult
end ; multiply
Related Links
  • call

    Execute the specified function or global ProcScript.

  • params…endparams

    Declare the parameter block for an operation, function, or global ProcScript.

  • variables…endvariables

    Define the variables for a ProcScript module or component.
